## [00:00 - 03:00]

Okay, let's talk about sales strategies, specifically during OpenCart. So these are just some sales strategies that we've developed over the years that you can use that will increase conversions during your launch. Remember, lots of people see the launch, they see putting out three pieces of pre-launch content, and then they just think that, boom, it's done. But really, your OpenCart is a sequence, and these are some of the things you can do during your sequence to help improve your results. So I'm all about having something to mail every day. During your OpenCart period, you want to mail the people on your launch list every single day, and it's nice if you have new things to mail to them. Like at the beginning, it's just we're open at the end, we're closing, but in between, it's great to have something to mail. So here's just some ideas. There's lots and lots of different things you can do, but one of them I love to do is a case studies page. So this is what our case studies page, this is a recent version of our case studies page, what it looks like, and I'll make it just a little bigger for you. So it's simply, we've got a countdown timer up at the top, and this image was taken when there was just an hour left in our OpenCart, but this is just a big, long scroll of all kinds of case studies. And so you can do whatever you want here, but just one case study after another, after another, after another, and they just keep on going for quite a long ways. Obviously we've got a lot of case studies. If you don't have a lot of case studies, then don't use them. This is something that hangs up a lot of people. They're like, well, I don't have case studies. Well, there are other ways to get proof, other ways to build a social proof and to build a proof. I've got them, so I use them, but you don't have, this isn't something you have to do. This is something that's great to do if you've got this asset. Remember, all through this course, I focused on using whatever assets you have. This is an asset I have in my business. Now, one thing I wanna point out, we have just a little bit of text up at the top, which hopefully encourages people to scroll through and find the ones that they think applied to them the best. And then you'll note a link below each one that goes over to the sales page or the sales video. So that's what you want. You want people to come here, you want them to pick and choose and find the ones that they like that resonate with them and watch them, but you always wanna have this opportunity to click on over to go check out your sales video or sales message. Another thing that we like to do, let me minimize this, is an all access page. So this is a page that has all your pre-launch material on one page.

## [03:00 - 06:03]

So we've got all our pre-launch videos here for people to watch. Again, with a link to click on over to get more details at your sales page. So again, this image was captured with just under an hour to go in our open cart. This page can be super effective if you're doing a joint venture launch, because what we do with this is we put this page together so our partners can mail on this page with no opt-in in front of it. And for me, for our launches, our partners do love to mail on this page because this is like an extra gift they can give to their people. It's like, hey, go check out this awesome free content and you don't even have to opt in for it. So really love to do our all access page, especially in joint venture launches. Another thing you could do is put out an additional video, an additional piece of content. We'll often do that during our cart week. Often do a live broadcast and you can pick whatever tool you wanna use. You can do that on Facebook, you could do it on Instagram, you could do it on YouTube, you can do it, there's all kinds of places. You can do live broadcasts, but you can just do a live broadcast. I usually don't like to do that on the closing day, maybe the day before your closing day, because on closing day, all roads lead to the sales page. So I don't like to muddy the waters by giving them a link to go watch a live broadcast on closing day. So maybe the day before closing day or two days before closing day, and this is you put, you do the live broadcast, you email out to your launch list and say, I'm gonna do a live broadcast. Often in cart week, that live broadcast focused on answering questions. So it could be a Q and A. And then another one you could do is what we call an unboxing or a product tour. So this could be, even if it's an online, if you're selling an online membership site, that's a great time to do a little screen capture where you go through the membership site and you show people what they can get. If it's a physical product, you can actually do an unboxing and show what it looks like. If it's an online course, you could go through the different modules and show what's coming. If it's going to be a live event, you could actually go to wherever the hotel, whatever the venue is and walk through it and just talk about it. So lots of different ways you can do this, but it can just be a nice thing to add into your cart week. Another thing you wanna pay attention to is your checkout page. So this is the page, once people are on your sales video or your sales letter, that they click to actually go to the page where they fill out their information and check out and order your product or service. So we have added a live chat component. We use livechatinc.com. And this is one of those deals. You've seen this on other checkout pages where there's a little icon on the page

## [06:03 - 09:05]

where they can click it to actually have a live chat. So in our last launch, our team answered 2,206 chats with a 97% satisfaction rate. Now, you might look at that and say, oh, well, I can't handle 2,000 chats, but you just have to remember that I'm playing at a pretty big, multimillion dollar scale. And if you have a smaller launch, you're probably not gonna have 2,000 chats. But when we go back and look at how did those chats convert into sales? It was very, very high, very, very high rate of conversion. Another thing you can do is have a countdown timer on the top of your checkout page. I don't recommend doing this until your last 48 hours. Because if you do it before that, I believe that you just give people a reason to procrastinate. But then in your last 40, actually, there are 36 hours. So if we're closing our cart on Friday, then on Thursday morning, we'll put that countdown timer on the top of our checkout page and our sales page. And then you need to think through your payment options. This is really part of your offer. It's not really a sales strategy during cart week, but I just thought I'd throw this in here because lots of times people, well, you will absolutely make more sales if you give some type of a payment plan. Absolutely 100%, you'll make more sales. Now those additional sales, I mean, there is a negative to that because you will not generally collect all the payments in your payment plan. And the people that buy through a payment plan will tend to refund at a higher rate. They tend to be, just to make a broad generalization, your absolute best clients will generally be the ones, the best meaning, the ones that take the most action, get the best results, become your star students, become your case studies. Those folks tend to be the single-pay people, but that doesn't mean great people don't come in through the payment plan as well. So this is a business decision, whether you want to have a payment plan, but you will make more sales. It just comes with a few more headaches. For abandoned cart strategy, so this is, you will have a lot of people that watch your sales video or read your sales letter, click on the link to go on over to your checkout page and not actually checkout. In fact, the majority of people will go there and I don't know if it's a curiosity thing or what, but the majority of people will click on over there and they will not actually buy. Now, in these days, the shopping cart systems have gotten so sophisticated that many of them allow you to actually track who has gone over there and actually tag those people.

So you can actually, if someone clicks on over, if you set up your tagging right, you can actually track that and know what email addresses of what people have clicked on over to your order page and not bought. ## [12:06 - 15:10]

And I just wanted to make sure you saw this earlier email, and here's the earlier email. And we did cut out the piece about the live chat in there, and we gave the link to the summer page, and that was it. So I mean, you can see these emails are super, super simple, super simple, not much to them. Let's take a look at what they did. We've got an extra 65 sales. So here's the subject lines. We didn't actually end up sending these two. And I'm not sure how we got two sales from these. So maybe this is really on 61 sales. But yeah, here we go. That first one, we've got 36 sales. 36 sales came from that one. 16 sales for the second one, and 15 sales from the third one. So when you look at how easy it was to put this copy together, was it worth it? Yes, it was absolutely worth putting that sequence together. The thing you want to think about is, and you want to think about this before you actually close, is what's going to happen when the cart closes, when your launch ends, when people click through the day after your launch closes, what's going to happen? And what you want is you want some kind of a program closed page with an opt-in form to register. Because what will happen is you'll be getting elevated levels of traffic for a number of days and even a number of weeks after your launch closes. So you want to capture those people. Because eventually, you're going to have another launch. You're going to have another promotion. Something's going to happen. Now, in my launches, my joint venture launches, where there's tens or hundreds of thousands, or possibly even millions of emails going around from my partners, there's lots of people that aren't going to get around to clicking until after the launch is over. That's just the reality. Sometimes people, an email might sit in someone's inbox for weeks, and then they decide to open it and click on through. So you want some type of a page to capture those opt-ins once they come on over to your page. And then finally, I want to talk about reopen strategies. A lot of people will have a reopen from their launch. So they go through their cart week. They open it, or however long. I typically will do a five-day open. You'll open it. Five days later, you close it. And then a lot of people, the next week or a few days later, they will have a reopen. And so there are pros and cons to this. In general, I really don't care for doing reopens, because if I go all out and I tell people the cart is closing and I send three or four emails on closing day, then it just really feels disingenuous a couple days later to say, oh, we decided to reopen. And so you're basically training people that, you know what? You really don't have to buy at the end of the cart, because there's going to be a reopen. And so a number of years ago, it seemed like it became

## [15:10 - 18:12]

sort of a standard that people were doing reopens, because the pro, the positive doing a reopen is that you will get about 10% to 15% more sales with no extra work. So just the next week, you say, maybe you close on Friday or whatever day of the week. Then the next Tuesday, you can send out an email that says, hey, tomorrow we're going to, we had a few more spots open up or whatever. You, we'll talk about that in just a minute, but we're going to reopen and it's gonna, we're gonna be taking new people for 24 hours or something like that. Again, I don't like to do this. I really don't like to do it. I really, really don't like to do it. It feels disingenuous, dishonest, but sometimes like a couple of years ago, we had massive shopping cart problems. Our shopping cart provider just really had serious problems on our closing day and we had a lot of people that couldn't buy. So in that case, we actually did do a reopen. So I talked a little about pros and cons. Pros as you make more money, cons is that, I think it's at the end of the day, unless there is a legitimate reason, I just think it's pretty disingenuous. And it trains your list and really the universe that he or she, well, they're not dead serious about their deadline. You can just don't bother with that deadline. You can, you always get in later. So I'm not a big fan of it. So if you do, you need a reason why. I think you need a legitimate reason why. I think something like technical issues at the end of your cart, I felt completely justified a few years ago when we did that because people couldn't get through. So what we did was we only emailed the people who are actually on our sales page on the last day. The people that we knew were directly impacted because a lot of them, when they clicked through the order page, they couldn't get through to the order page because of our technical issues with our shopping cart provider. So we had a real reason and we told people the reason in our email and then we opened up for about a 24 hour period. We made about some like the typical between 10 and 15% more sales. Partners, are you gonna invite your partners to go ahead and promote? And again, this is, if you ask your partners to go in a joint venture launch, if you ask your partners to go out all crazy for your clothes and then you turn around and say, oh, by the way, we weren't really super serious about that close. So why don't you email this week? Well, you could do that, but we don't ever do that. Again, just though it's a long run thinking. I don't wanna create this reputation for someone who just, your deadlines aren't serious. Then timing, generally when we do a reopen, it's just for a day or maybe two days and it's maybe the next week. And yeah, so for timing and duration, maybe it's three or four days after the original close

## [18:12 - 18:32]

and it's only for 24, maybe 36, maybe 48 hours in duration. So be really careful with the reopen. I'm super, if I haven't given you the impression yet, I'm super not a fan of them, don't like them at all. But sometimes if there's a legitimate reason, they, it can be a cool thing to do.
